cataloging_subject.label: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The witness-specific Middle English text of the Northern Octavian represented by Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, ff. 90rb-102vb, and by the repository item OctC. This cataloging subject is one complete Cambridge text of the romance, not the abstract Northern version across all witnesses, the distinct Southern version, the whole Cambridge manuscript, McSparran's working transcription, the 1986 parallel EETS edition, the OTA package containing both Cambridge and Lincoln texts, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed c. 1350; Cambridge witness copied in the middle of the fifteenth century
catalog_summary.region: England; Northeast Midlands, perhaps Yorkshire, for composition; witness scribal language separately associated with Leicestershire

titles.value: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, combining DIMEV's established version title with an explicit witness qualifier

assertion.notes: The qualifier prevents the 1,731-line Cambridge redaction from being mistaken for every witness of Northern Octavian.
This broader title is retained as an authority form; the manifest's subject remains the Cambridge witness-specific text.

titles.value: lytyll & mykyll olde & yonge / lystenyth now to my talkynge
Jhesu lorde heuyn kynge / graunt vs all thy blessyng / and yn heuyn to abyde

notes: No medieval author is securely identified. Older proposed common authorship with Sir Isumbras is not treated as a personal attribution.

assertion.notes: The Northern and Southern English versions independently abbreviate and adapt an Old French Octavian; this role does not identify the Cambridge copyist.

scope.description: Direct working transcription of the Cambridge witness underlying the OctC encoding

notes: The XML source description calls this a transcription, not an edition, and distinguishes it from McSparran's published EETS edition.

scope.description: 1986 parallel EETS edition, consulted by OctC only for comparison pagination
Later Lincoln-based comparison edition, second edition 2006

assertion.notes: The approximate scholarly date is not converted into an invented exact normalized interval.

scope.description: Date of the Cambridge witness preserving this text

assertion.notes: Witness production is distinct from the romance's earlier composition.
A composition localization for the Northern version, not the known location of the Cambridge manuscript or an automatic description of both of its scribes.
Both sources qualify Yorkshire with uncertainty; it is not promoted to a certain origin.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English cataloging prose and supplied XML headings are editorial layers, not additional languages of the medieval work.

form_statements.description: Narrative verse, mostly in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas with six-line stanzas also recorded by DIMEV. The repository XML preserves 1,731 lines but does not encode stanza groups.

assertion.notes: Florent's noble self-discovery, giant combat, knighting, tournaments, warfare, and courtly marriage are central rather than incidental.

subject_statements.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion

assertion.notes: Includes the false adultery accusation, separated twins, foundling and fosterage episodes, and eventual recognition.
The contrast between Florent's innate nobility and Clement's bourgeois and mercantile values is a sustained organizing theme.

summaries.value: After seven childless years, the emperor Octavian's wife bears twin sons. The emperor's mother frames the empress for adultery, and mother and children are exiled rather than burned. In the wilderness the twins are carried away by animals. The empress reaches Jerusalem with one child, the younger Octavian, while the other is sold to the Parisian burgess Clement and raised as Florent. Florent's noble instincts conflict comically with Clement's mercantile values; he defeats a Saracen giant, is recognized by his father, and loves the sultan's daughter Marsabelle. When the Christian rulers are captured, the younger Octavian travels from Jerusalem with his mother and her lioness, defeats the Saracens, and reunites the family. Marsabelle converts and marries Florent, and the emperor's treacherous mother kills herself.

The Cambridge text is a shorter redaction of Northern Octavian, omitting passages retained by the Lincoln witness. Its 1,731-line direct transcription is cataloged here as a witness-specific text. The 18 supplied hundred-line XML divisions and 52 even EETS page milestones are navigation and comparison structures, not intrinsic medieval chapters or stanza counts.

extent.description: The XML has 1,731 consecutively numbered L elements, independently matching DIMEV and the Database of Middle English Romance for the Cambridge witness. It has 18 DIV1 elements labeled as supplied line groups, 19 HEAD elements, 52 PB milestones carrying every even EETS page number from 76 through 178, 53 EPB elements, and no LG or NOTE elements. Consequently no canonical section or stanza total is asserted; the 18 divisions and 52 page breaks are representation metrics. XPTR/@TO values exhibit partial cross-version alignment behavior in an undocumented namespace and are not used to calculate extent.

content_parts.label: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
content_parts.summary: The complete Cambridge redaction of Northern Octavian, from the opening call to listeners through the reunited family's return to Rome and final prayer.

locators.value: ff. 90rb-102vb; f. 90r is shared with the end of Sir Tryamour and f. 102v with the opening of Bevis of Hampton
complete Cambridge Octavian transcription, 1,731 lines; carrier and internal pagination not identified
even pp. 76-178; used by OctC only as supplied comparison pagination
octc-1683.txt; the same OTA package also contains collateral Lincoln component octl-1683.txt

notes: DIMEV supplies the column-level boundary; DMR independently corroborates the folio range and 1,731-line extent at folio-side granularity.
The XML does not encode the manuscript's scribal hand change, stanza spaces, or physical leaf boundaries.

related_works.title: Octavian (Northern version)
notes: The Cambridge text is one complete witness-specific redaction of the Northern version; Lincoln and the Wynkyn de Worde fragment preserve other states.

related_works.title: Octavian (Southern version)
notes: A distinct independent Middle English adaptation in six-line tail-rhyme, not another label for the Cambridge text.

related_works.title: Octavian (Old French couplet romance)
notes: Hudson describes the Northern and Southern English redactors as independently abbreviating the same thirteenth-century Old French romance; no surviving French manuscript is asserted as the direct physical exemplar of the Cambridge copy.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/OctC.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER; TEXT/BODY; DIV1 supplied line groups; PB and L elements
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the OctC identity, exact title, Frances McSparran responsibility, direct-transcription statement, 1993 U-M distribution statement, embedded OTA restriction and identifier, 1350-1375 profile date, transcription policy, EETS comparison pagination, and complete line text.

evidence.citation: XPath, hash, and Git-object inspection of OctC.xml.
evidence.locator: counts and attributes of DIV1, HEAD, L, PB, EPB, LG, NOTE, P, and XPTR
evidence.summary: Finds a 160,248-byte file with 18 supplied DIV1 groups, 19 HEAD, 1,731 L, 52 PB, 53 EPB, 0 LG, 0 NOTE, 5 header P, and 1,731 XPTR elements. L/@N runs 0001-1731; PB/@N runs through every even page 76-178. OctC has 331 XPTR TO=0000 values and 1,400 nonzero occurrences resolving to 1,397 unique values; comparison with OctL shows partial overlap rather than a shared exhaustive line-number universe, so the undocumented TO namespace is not used for extent. The Git blob is 23ab964fbecf7a0ea9b92feae1d3a319dbb8c816.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, record 3132 and manuscript key CULFf238.
evidence.summary: Supplies the established Northern-version title, IMEV/NIMEV 1918, romance genre, twelve- and six-line stanza forms and rhyme patterns, Cambridge incipit and explicit, exact ff. 90rb-102vb locus, 1,731-line extent, Scolar facsimile reference, and McSparran even pp. 76-178; it distinguishes Southern record 2930.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Octavian.
evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for c. 1350 composition, Northern Northeast Midlands and possible Yorkshire localization, Northern and Southern tail-rhyme forms, IMEV identifiers, plot, subjects, Cambridge and Lincoln witnesses, early print, and modern editions.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.2.38.
evidence.locator: manuscript description and contents, especially Octavian at ff. 90r-102v
evidence.summary: Supports the mid-fifteenth-century paper witness, 297 x 206 mm dimensions, two scribes with the second taking over in the middle of Octavian, complete manuscript contents, shared work boundaries, Northern identity, 1,731 lines, and 1979 Scolar facsimile.

evidence.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., Four Middle English Romances, 2nd ed. (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2006), general introduction pp. 1-3 and Introduction to Octavian pp. 39-43.
evidence.summary: Supports anonymous authorship, composition range and localization, family-romance and tail-rhyme classification, Old French adaptation, plot and subjects, the distinction between Northern and Southern versions, the Cambridge text's shorter redaction, written relation to Lincoln, and manuscript-versus-editorial stanza formatting. Its manuscript list mislabels Cambridge as Southern and gives ff. 90r-101v; those isolated errors are not adopted.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. 2 (Cambridge, 1857), pp. 404-408, no. 1205, Ff.II.38.
evidence.summary: Official historical catalog evidence for the 247-leaf paper manuscript, folio format, double columns of about forty lines, mid-fifteenth-century dating, losses, and item 37, an untitled tale of Emperor Octavian.

Confirms the public current OTA package, contributor John Price-Wilkin, 1992 issue date, CC BY 3.0 item label, and three-file inventory containing header1683.xml plus separate OctC Cambridge and OctL Lincoln text components.

evidence.citation: Frances McSparran, ed., Octovian, EETS Original Series 289 (London and New York: Oxford University Press for EETS, 1986).
evidence.summary: Confirms title, editor, publisher, date, series number, ISBN, 244-page extent, and parallel Cambridge and Lincoln transcriptions; DIMEV supplies the even/odd page allocation used by the XML.

open_questions.question: Can the Northern version's Northeast Midlands, perhaps Yorkshire, composition localization be reconciled more precisely with Ff.2.38's Leicestershire-associated LALME profile and its two scribes without transferring witness language to the anonymous composer?
next_steps: Locate the exact hand-change line within Octavian by consulting the manuscript or complete facsimile.
Review LALME/eLALME LP 531 and scholarship assigning forms to each Ff.2.38 hand.
Keep composition, exemplar, scribal dialect, and manuscript-production place as separate claims.

open_questions.question: What are the exact current foliation, column, line, quire, and scribal-hand boundaries of the Cambridge Octavian text, including its shared opening and closing folios?
next_steps: Obtain an authorized complete Ff.2.38 facsimile or inspect the manuscript and 1979 Scolar facsimile.
Verify ff. 90rb-102vb against current foliation and map the second scribe's takeover line.
Document the Sir Tryamour-to-Octavian and Octavian-to-Bevis shared-surface transitions.

open_questions.question: How do OctC L/@N values, XPTR targets, McSparran's even Cambridge pages, Lincoln cross-references, and stanza boundaries align line by line?
next_steps: Compare the complete OTA OctC and OctL components and the 1986 EETS parallel edition under their applicable terms.
Map the XML's zero and shifted XPTR targets without treating them as missing verse lines.
Add stanza metrics only after manuscript and editorial boundaries are explicitly crosswalked.

notes: DIMEV's exact ff. 90rb-102vb and 1,731-line record controls the work boundary; DMR independently corroborates ff. 90r-102v and 1,731 lines.

Hudson's prose correctly identifies the Cambridge text as Northern and shorter than Lincoln, but the manuscript list on printed p. 43 accidentally labels Cambridge Southern and ends it at f. 101v. Those isolated list entries conflict with DIMEV, DMR, the XML ending, and Hudson's own discussion and are not adopted.

The 1857 CUL catalog calls the manuscript hand uniform, whereas the current DMR handlist reports two scribes with the second taking over during Octavian. Both attributed statements remain visible; the current specialist description is preferred pending direct inspection.
The XML header's 1993 date is a U-M distribution/publication-layer date, not the medieval composition date or the OTA package's 1992 issue date.
No Leicestershire place statement is assigned to the work: the DIMEV/LALME localization belongs to manuscript scribal language and may span more than one hand.
